core_behavior.note: All three models describe a multi-phase reset flow but none specifies the reset mechanism (email link vs. code vs. other) as a deliberate open choice, and none flags the email-existence confirmation as a security tradeoff requiring a decision.

inputs.note: All three models list email/username, token, new password, and confirmation as inputs with minor wording differences.

outputs.note: All three models include a confirmation message, reset email, success message, and error messages as outputs.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes email or phone as channels and a 30-minute expiry; Model B assumes email only with hashed token storage; Model C assumes email only with a 24-hour expiry — differing on channel, expiry duration, and token storage design not constrained by the requirement.

failure_modes.note: Model A reveals whether an email exists in its error message; Models B and C specify a generic message to avoid account enumeration, representing incompatible error-response behaviors.

edge_cases.note: Model A covers no-registered-email, locked accounts, and multiple requests; Model B covers inactive accounts and session invalidation; Model C covers whitespace input, multiple reset emails, and reused tokens — each model introduces different edge cases not specified by the requirement, and rate limiting on multiple requests is noted but not fully specified by any model.

security_constraints.note: Model B specifies hashed token storage, session invalidation on reset, max password length to prevent bcrypt DoS, and explicit account-enumeration prevention; Model A specifies rate limiting on reset requests (5/hour) but does not hash tokens or invalidate sessions; Model C specifies audit logging but omits session invalidation and token hashing — representing materially different security postures; rate limiting is absent from Models B and C.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies a 2-second response time threshold; Model C specifies email delivery within 5 minutes; Model B only mentions a 128-character password length limit as a performance constraint — the models specify different thresholds and metrics.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) appeared most frequently, tied across two fields, reflecting that the underspecified requirement led models to independently introduce different design choices for token expiry, delivery channel, and edge-case handling.
